md: ## Two ways to look at a number
Every counting number can be viewed two ways:
**As a multiple** of smaller numbers — what numbers divide INTO it.
**As a building block** for larger numbers — what numbers it divides INTO.
These two perspectives lead to **factors** and **multiples** — and once you have them, GCD and LCM follow naturally.

blocks.title: Factor vs multiple — same relationship, two angles
blocks.body: $3$ is a **factor** of $12$ because $3$ divides $12$ evenly. $12$ is a **multiple** of $3$ because $12 = 3 \cdot 4$. The same fact, looked at from each end.

md: ### Listing factors
**Factors of $24$:** $1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24$. (Trick: pair them — $1 \cdot 24$, $2 \cdot 12$, $3 \cdot 8$, $4 \cdot 6$. Once you reach a pair where the factors meet, you're done.)
**Factors of $36$:** $1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 9, 12, 18, 36$. (Note $6 \cdot 6 = 36$ — this is why $36$ has an *odd* number of factors. All perfect squares do.)

blocks.title: Divisibility rules — quick checks
blocks.body: **$2$:** ends in $0, 2, 4, 6, 8$. **$3$:** digit sum is divisible by $3$ ($1+2+3=6$, so $123$ is divisible by $3$). **$5$:** ends in $0$ or $5$. **$9$:** digit sum is divisible by $9$. **$10$:** ends in $0$. **$4$:** last two digits divisible by $4$. **$6$:** divisible by both $2$ and $3$.

md: ## Prime numbers — the atoms
A **prime** is a number greater than $1$ whose only factors are $1$ and itself.
$2$ is the only **even** prime — every other even number has $2$ as a factor.
$1$ is **not** prime (by convention — and for very good reasons that show up in Algebra II).

blocks.title: Why primes matter
blocks.body: Every counting number greater than $1$ can be written as a product of primes in **exactly one way** (up to order). This is the **Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic** — primes are the indivisible atoms of multiplication.

md: ## Prime factorization — the factor tree
To find a number's prime factorization, keep splitting until every leaf is prime.
**Example:** $60$.
$60 = 6 \cdot 10 = (2 \cdot 3)(2 \cdot 5) = 2 \cdot 2 \cdot 3 \cdot 5 = 2^2 \cdot 3 \cdot 5$.
It doesn't matter which split you make first — the final list of primes is always the same.

**Try it yourself.** Drag the slider (or type a number) to see its factor tree. Notice: **prime** numbers are single orange dots — no splits to make. **Perfect squares** (like 36, 49, 64) make tidy symmetric trees.
## GCD — the biggest factor in common
The **GCD** (Greatest Common Divisor) of two numbers, also called **GCF** (Greatest Common Factor), is the largest number that divides both.
Common factors: $1, 2, 3, 6$. **Biggest is $6$**, so $\gcd(12, 18) = 6$.

blocks.title: GCD via prime factorization
blocks.body:
Write each number's prime factorization. For each prime, take the **smaller** exponent. Multiply.

$12 = 2^2 \cdot 3$. $\;18 = 2 \cdot 3^2$. Take $\min$ of each exponent: $2^1 \cdot 3^1 = 6$. ✓

md: ### When does GCD show up?
**Simplifying fractions.** $\dfrac{18}{24}$: $\gcd(18, 24) = 6$, so $\dfrac{18}{24} = \dfrac{3}{4}$.
**Simplifying ratios.** $24 : 36 \Rightarrow$ divide both by $\gcd = 12 \Rightarrow 2 : 3$.
**Tile problems.** 'Largest square tile that fits a $24 \times 36$ floor exactly?' Answer: $\gcd(24, 36) = 12$ inches.
## LCM — the smallest multiple in common
The **LCM** (Least Common Multiple) of two numbers is the smallest number both divide into.
**Example:** $\mathrm{lcm}(4, 6)$.
Multiples of $4$: $4, 8, 12, 16, 20, 24, \ldots$
Multiples of $6$: $6, 12, 18, 24, \ldots$
Smallest in both lists: **$12$**. So $\mathrm{lcm}(4, 6) = 12$.

blocks.title: LCM via prime factorization
blocks.body:
Write each number's prime factorization. For each prime, take the **larger** exponent. Multiply.

$4 = 2^2$. $\;6 = 2 \cdot 3$. Take $\max$ of each exponent: $2^2 \cdot 3 = 12$. ✓

md: ### When does LCM show up?
**Adding fractions.** $\dfrac{1}{4} + \dfrac{1}{6}$: common denominator is $\mathrm{lcm}(4, 6) = 12$.
**Cycle / synchronization problems.** 'Two clocks chime every $12$ and $18$ minutes. They just chimed together — when next?' Answer: $\mathrm{lcm}(12, 18) = 36$ minutes.

blocks.title: The beautiful identity: $\gcd(a, b) \cdot \mathrm{lcm}(a, b) = a \cdot b$
blocks.body: Once you know one of them, you get the other for free. $\gcd(12, 18) = 6$ and $12 \cdot 18 = 216$, so $\mathrm{lcm}(12, 18) = 216 / 6 = 36$. ✓

md: ### Reading GCD & LCM straight from the trees
**Step 1.** Build the prime factor tree of each number.
**Step 2.** For each prime that appears in **at least one** tree, count how many times it shows up in each.
**Step 3.** **GCD** uses only primes that appear in **every** tree (green below) — take the **smallest** count.
**Step 4.** **LCM** uses every prime that appears anywhere — take the **largest** count.
**Step 5.** Multiply your selected primes together.

blocks.title: Worked example: $\gcd(12, 18)$ and $\mathrm{lcm}(12, 18)$
blocks.body: $12 = 2^2 \cdot 3$ and $18 = 2 \cdot 3^2$. Both trees share **2 and 3** (green). For GCD take the smaller exponent of each: $\min(2,1) = 1$ for $2$, and $\min(1,2) = 1$ for $3$ → $\gcd = 2 \cdot 3 = 6$. For LCM take the larger: $\max(2,1) = 2$ for $2$, and $\max(1,2) = 2$ for $3$ → $\mathrm{lcm} = 2^2 \cdot 3^2 = 36$. Slide the numbers below to test other pairs.

md: ### Three numbers — the rule still works

Same algorithm — just check more trees. A prime contributes to GCD only if it appears in **all three**. Try this set: $12$, $18$, $24$ (the kind of group you'd see when adding three fractions with different denominators).

blocks.title: Watch out: the $\gcd \cdot \mathrm{lcm} = a \cdot b$ identity is for TWO numbers only
blocks.body: It does NOT generalize to three or more. For $12, 18, 24$: $\gcd = 6$, $\mathrm{lcm} = 72$, $\gcd \cdot \mathrm{lcm} = 432$, but $12 \cdot 18 \cdot 24 = 5184$. Different numbers entirely.

md: ## Mental shortcuts to remember
**For two numbers where one divides the other:** GCD is the smaller, LCM is the larger. $\gcd(6, 24) = 6$, $\mathrm{lcm}(6, 24) = 24$.
**For two coprime numbers** (no common factors besides $1$): GCD $= 1$, LCM $= a \cdot b$. $\gcd(4, 9) = 1$, $\mathrm{lcm}(4, 9) = 36$.
**For larger numbers:** prime-factorize and take min/max exponents. Always works.

blocks.title: Common mistake
blocks.body: Don't confuse GCD and LCM. **GCD is small** (a divisor — never bigger than the inputs). **LCM is big** (a multiple — never smaller than the larger input). 'Smallest common multiple' and 'largest common divisor' — the *adjective* tells you which side.
